narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, like your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, which is right around the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset, and your strong first-year school decile ranking of 8, well above the average of about 5.7. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 43% chance that you would fail it—high enough to trigger a rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score of 26, significantly below the dataset average of 36.7, which signaled to the model a notable risk despite your other strengths. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your excellent first-year decile of 8, which strongly suggested potential for success and pulled the prediction toward passing. Your undergraduate GPA also helped in that direction, as its near-average performance indicated steady academic capability without major red flags. Interestingly, your lower third-year decile of 3, below the average of 5.5, played a lesser role here, but it didn't outweigh the LSAT's impact. Full-time enrollment status, common for over 92% of students, was neutral in this decision. Compared to typical applicants who get approved, your profile mirrors many in GPA and background but falls short on LSAT, where stronger scores often tip the scales toward predicted bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your overall potential as a dedicated student—it's the model weighing these patterns from past bar-takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above average, as it carries the heaviest weight; strengthening later-year performance could help too. You might also explore part-time options or programs with holistic reviews that value your early academic promise more flexibly. With targeted improvements, a stronger application could flip this prediction in your favor next time. Keep pushing—you've already shown resilience in your first-year results.
